meta.title: OAI-2: Prompt Design, Safety and Agent Workflows

questions.stem:
A marketing manager asks ChatGPT to "write something about our new product" and receives a vague two-sentence response that doesn't match the company's needs.

What is the MOST likely cause of this poor output?

options.text: Marketing prompts require a paid ChatGPT subscription
ChatGPT is not capable of writing marketing content
The prompt lacks specificity about audience, tone, format, and product details
The model's temperature setting is too low

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because vague prompts produce vague outputs. Without specifying the target audience, desired tone, output format (e.g., social post vs. email), and product details, the model has no basis for producing useful content.

**A** is incorrect because ChatGPT is well-suited for marketing copywriting when given sufficient direction.

**C** is incorrect because temperature affects creativity and randomness but does not cause a short or off-target response from an under-specified prompt.

**D** is incorrect because prompt specificity is a technique issue unrelated to subscription tier.

[OpenAI Prompt Engineering Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering)

questions.stem:
A software engineer wants ChatGPT to consistently format all its code review feedback as a numbered list with a severity label (Critical / Major / Minor) before each item.

Where should this formatting requirement be placed for the MOST reliable results?

options.text: At the end of every user message as a reminder
In the system prompt so it applies to every response
In a separate follow-up message after receiving the initial response
In the code comments submitted for review

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the system prompt establishes persistent instructions that govern all responses in the session. Formatting rules placed there are applied consistently without needing to repeat them.

**A** is incorrect because embedding the requirement in every user message is repetitive and easy to forget; it also adds noise to the actual content of the request.

**C** is incorrect because asking for a reformat after receiving a response creates extra steps and is not scalable across many reviews.

**D** is incorrect because code comments are part of the content being reviewed, not instructions to the model.

[OpenAI Prompt Engineering Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering)

questions.stem:
A legal analyst needs to extract contract risk clauses and receives inconsistent results — sometimes bullet points, sometimes paragraphs, sometimes nothing relevant.

Which prompting change is MOST likely to improve consistency?

options.text: Provide a labeled example of an ideal extraction in the prompt (few-shot)
Submit the contract in multiple shorter messages instead of one
Switch to a different AI model
Ask the model to "try harder" in the prompt

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because few-shot examples give the model a concrete template to follow, dramatically reducing output variability. When you show the desired format with a sample input/output pair, the model learns the expected structure.

**A** is incorrect because instructing the model to "try harder" provides no structural guidance and will not resolve format inconsistency.

**C** is incorrect because splitting a contract into chunks may lose cross-clause context and does not address the format problem.

**D** is incorrect because the root cause is the underspecified prompt, not model capability; switching models without fixing the prompt will likely produce similar variability.

[OpenAI Prompt Engineering Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering)

questions.stem:
A human resources professional is using ChatGPT to draft interview questions for a senior data analyst role. The first draft contains generic questions suitable for any role.

Which prompt revision would MOST improve relevance?

options.text: Ask for exactly 20 questions instead of the current number
Request that the model search the internet for interview templates
Add "make it better" to the original prompt
Specify the seniority level, key responsibilities, required technical skills, and the company's interview philosophy

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because providing role-specific context — seniority, key responsibilities, required skills, and interview style — gives the model the information it needs to produce targeted, relevant questions.

**A** is incorrect because "make it better" is as vague as the original prompt and gives the model no direction.

**C** is incorrect because changing the quantity does not improve relevance; you will get 20 generic questions instead of fewer generic ones.

**D** is incorrect because standard ChatGPT does not browse the internet (unless browsing is enabled), and the issue is prompt specificity, not content sourcing.

[OpenAI Prompt Engineering Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering)

questions.stem:
A product manager wants ChatGPT to adopt the persona of a skeptical investor when critiquing a startup pitch deck. The initial system prompt says: "You are an investor. Critique this pitch."

After several turns, the model's critiques become increasingly mild and supportive rather than skeptical.

What is the MOST likely cause and fix?

options.text: Reduce the temperature setting so the model stops being creative with its feedback
The model cannot maintain personas; use a different tool
The system prompt persona is too vague; reinforce skepticism with specific behavioral instructions and examples of challenging questions
The pitch deck content is too positive, causing the model to agree with it

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a thin persona instruction like "You are an investor" lacks behavioral anchoring. Adding specific behaviors (e.g., "Always identify three weaknesses before any strength", "Challenge every financial projection") and sample challenging questions prevents the model from drifting toward agreeable responses over multi-turn conversations.

**A** is incorrect because ChatGPT is capable of maintaining personas when they are well-specified; the issue is prompt design, not model capability.

**C** is incorrect because the model should critique based on investor reasoning, not match the sentiment of the content being reviewed.

**D** is incorrect because lowering temperature reduces variability but does not restore a drifting persona; that requires better system prompt specification.

[OpenAI Prompt Engineering Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering)

questions.stem:
A customer success manager receives a ChatGPT response that is mostly correct but too long and includes unnecessary background information.

What is the BEST follow-up action?

options.text: Start a new conversation and try a completely different prompt
Follow up in the same conversation asking the model to shorten the response and remove the background section
Accept the response as-is since AI cannot be refined
Switch to a different AI vendor

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because iterative refinement in the same conversation is the standard workflow for improving AI outputs. The model retains context and can trim or restructure its previous response when given specific follow-up instructions.

**A** is incorrect because starting over discards the conversational context and the partially useful output; follow-up prompting is more efficient.

**C** is incorrect because AI outputs are meant to be refined through iteration; accepting a suboptimal response is not a best practice.

**D** is incorrect because length and relevance issues are addressed through prompt refinement, not by changing vendors.

[OpenAI Prompt Engineering Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering)

questions.stem:
A financial services analyst needs ChatGPT to generate investment summaries strictly based on provided data, with no speculation or forward-looking statements.

Which prompt instruction is MOST effective at enforcing this constraint?

options.text: "Use only the data provided below. Do not infer trends, make predictions, or include any information not explicitly stated in the data."
"Keep it factual"
"Avoid being too creative"
"Write a conservative summary"

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because it explicitly names the prohibited behaviors (inferring trends, making predictions, adding external information) and anchors the model to a specific data source. Explicit negative constraints are more reliable than vague positive directives.

**A** is incorrect because "conservative" is ambiguous and does not prevent the model from speculating.

**C** is incorrect because "keep it factual" is too vague and the model may still include inferred trends it considers factual.

**D** is incorrect because creativity restrictions do not specifically prevent financial speculation.

[OpenAI Prompt Engineering Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering)

questions.stem:
A content creator wants ChatGPT to generate five distinct headline variations for a blog post about remote work productivity. The first attempt produces five headlines with nearly identical wording and structure.

What should the content creator do to get genuinely diverse options?

options.text: Tell the model its previous headlines were bad
Use a longer prompt with more background about the blog post
Ask for 10 headlines instead of 5
Explicitly request varied formats such as a question, a how-to, a listicle teaser, a provocative statement, and a data-driven headline

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because specifying distinct formats forces structural diversity. Without explicit format constraints, the model defaults to similar patterns that scored well during training.

**A** is incorrect because doubling the count without format guidance will produce 10 similarly structured headlines.

**C** is incorrect because negative feedback alone ("those were bad") gives the model no direction on what "good" looks like; it may just produce different but still similar options.

**D** is incorrect because more background context helps with accuracy but does not drive format diversity.

[OpenAI Prompt Engineering Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/prompt-engineering)

questions.stem:
A research analyst uses ChatGPT to summarize a 50-page industry report and asks: "Summarize this report." The model produces a summary that seems accurate but omits several findings the analyst considers important.

What should the analyst do to get a more complete summary?

options.text: Accept the summary as complete since AI selects the most important points
Ask the model to rewrite the summary from scratch
Specify which sections or topics are most important and ask the model to ensure those are covered
Submit the report in multiple separate messages

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because AI models make their own judgments about salience when given open-ended summarization instructions. By explicitly naming the sections or topics that must appear, the analyst overrides the model's defaults and gets a summary aligned with her priorities.

**A** is incorrect because rewriting from scratch without new instructions will likely produce a similar selection of topics.

**C** is incorrect because splitting the report into separate messages loses cross-section context and complicates the task.

**D** is incorrect because AI salience judgments may not match domain expert priorities; specifying requirements is essential for professional use.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A product manager receives a ChatGPT response that states: "Studies show that 78% of remote workers are more productive than office workers." She cannot find a source for this statistic.

What is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Use the statistic as-is because ChatGPT only produces accurate information
Ask ChatGPT to provide the source and independently verify the statistic before using it
Discard the entire ChatGPT response because one unsourced statistic invalidates everything
Rephrase the statistic as "studies suggest" to make it sound less definitive

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because AI models can hallucinate plausible-sounding statistics. Asking for the source tests whether the model can substantiate the claim, and then independently verifying it ensures accuracy before professional use.

**A** is incorrect because ChatGPT can and does produce inaccurate statistics; blindly trusting numerical claims is a significant risk.

**C** is incorrect because one questionable statistic does not invalidate all other content; evaluate claims individually and fact-check as needed.

**D** is incorrect because hedging language does not fix a false statistic; the underlying accuracy problem remains.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A business development executive asks ChatGPT to draft a competitive analysis comparing her company to three competitors. She provides no background about her company or the competitors.

What is the MOST likely problem with the output?

options.text: The model will generate plausible-sounding but potentially inaccurate information about the competitors because it lacks grounding data
The output will be in a foreign language
The model cannot compare more than two companies at once
The model will refuse to write competitive analyses

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because without grounding data, the model will draw on its training knowledge, which may be outdated, incomplete, or fabricated. Competitive intelligence requires current, sourced data — something the model cannot reliably provide from memory alone.

**A** is incorrect because competitive analysis is a common and accepted use case for ChatGPT.

**C** is incorrect because the model responds in the language of the prompt by default.

**D** is incorrect because the model can compare multiple entities; the issue is data quality, not quantity.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A technical writer asks ChatGPT to format its output as a table with columns: Feature, Description, and Availability. The model consistently returns bullet points instead.

Which approach is MOST likely to solve this?

options.text: Submit the content in a CSV file for the model to convert
Use bold text in the prompt to emphasize the table request
Mention the format request at the very start of the prompt before any other content, and include an explicit example of the table header row
Ask the model to use a spreadsheet application instead

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because placing the format requirement prominently at the beginning of the prompt — before the content — and showing an example header row (Feature | Description | Availability) gives the model unambiguous early signal about the expected structure.

**B** is incorrect because ChatGPT cannot open spreadsheet applications; the table formatting happens within the chat response.

**C** is incorrect because uploading a CSV is a different workflow and does not resolve an instruction-following issue with the current prompt.

**D** is incorrect because markdown bold in a prompt does not function as an emphasis signal to the model's instruction-following mechanism.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A financial analyst asks ChatGPT to review its own analysis of a quarterly earnings report and identify any errors or unsupported claims.

Which limitation should the analyst MOST be aware of when using this approach?

options.text: Self-review prompts are banned by OpenAI usage policy
ChatGPT cannot read its own previous messages
The model may systematically miss the same errors in self-review that it made in the original output, since it uses the same underlying reasoning
Self-review always produces perfect error detection in AI systems

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a model that generated an error often applies the same flawed reasoning when reviewing that output. Self-review catches surface-level issues (formatting, obvious logical gaps) but is unreliable for detecting systematic factual errors or knowledge gaps the model does not know it has.

**A** is incorrect because the model can read all messages in the current context window, including its own prior responses.

**C** is incorrect because self-review is a useful but imperfect technique; it should complement, not replace, human review for high-stakes outputs.

**D** is incorrect because asking a model to review its output is a standard and permitted technique.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A marketing team lead asks ChatGPT to write a product description in a "professional but friendly" tone. The output sounds stiff and formal.

What additional context would MOST help the model match the desired tone?

options.text: Tell the model to use more adjectives
Provide two or three example sentences from past brand communications that exemplify the tone, and describe the target customer
Ask the model to write it again but shorter
Request a bulleted version of the description

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because tone is subjective and hard to define verbally. Providing concrete examples from existing brand materials gives the model a calibration target, while describing the customer helps it pitch vocabulary and familiarity level appropriately.

**A** is incorrect because adding adjectives does not address formality; a stiff response can include many adjectives.

**C** is incorrect because shortening the response does not change its tone register.

**D** is incorrect because bullet format does not affect whether the language reads as professional-but-friendly.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A healthcare communications manager asks ChatGPT to explain the benefits of a new medication. The model produces a confident-sounding explanation that includes a mechanism of action that the manager cannot verify in the prescribing information.

What should the manager do?

options.text: Cross-reference the specific claim against the official prescribing information and consult a medical professional before publishing
Ask ChatGPT to confirm that the information is correct
Remove the mechanism of action section and publish the rest without verification
Publish the explanation as-is because medical AI is always accurate

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because healthcare communications carry significant risk of harm from inaccuracies. The AI may have hallucinated a plausible mechanism, and the prescribing information is the authoritative source. A medical professional review adds a necessary quality gate before publication.

**A** is incorrect because AI medical information must always be verified against authoritative sources; overconfidence in AI accuracy is a significant risk in healthcare contexts.

**C** is incorrect because asking the model to confirm its own output does not constitute verification — it will typically affirm its response using the same underlying (possibly flawed) reasoning.

**D** is incorrect because removing one unverified section does not mean the remaining content is accurate; full verification is required for healthcare communications.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A communications specialist wants ChatGPT to produce a 150-word executive summary. The model consistently returns 400-word summaries.

What is the MOST direct fix?

options.text: Manually trim the response to 150 words
Use a different AI tool for length-controlled writing
Ask the model to count words as it writes
Add "Respond in exactly 150 words" to the prompt and verify the length after generation

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because an explicit word count instruction in the prompt is the most direct control mechanism. Models do not always hit exact word counts precisely, so verifying length after generation and iterating is a standard workflow.

**A** is incorrect because instructing the model to count as it writes is not a reliable mechanism; models generate text, not count in real time.

**C** is incorrect because manual trimming works but is time-consuming and bypasses the prompt improvement that would make future requests more efficient.

**D** is incorrect because length control is a common feature of all major language models and is addressed through prompt design.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
An employee at a law firm wants to use ChatGPT to review a confidential client contract and identify potential risk clauses.

What is the MOST important consideration before proceeding?

options.text: Whether the client has a preferred AI vendor
Whether ChatGPT has enough legal knowledge to read contracts
Whether sharing confidential client data with a third-party AI service complies with the firm's data handling policies, client confidentiality agreements, and applicable regulations
Whether the contract is longer than ChatGPT's context window

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because law firms are bound by client confidentiality obligations, and submitting client data to a third-party AI service may constitute a disclosure breach under bar rules, engagement agreements, or privacy laws. This determination must precede any use.

**A** is incorrect because legal knowledge capability is secondary to the fundamental question of whether using the tool is permissible.

**C** is incorrect because context length is a practical consideration that comes after the legal and ethical question is resolved.

**D** is incorrect because client AI vendor preference is not typically part of engagement agreements and is not the governing consideration.

[OpenAI Usage Policies](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/safety-best-practices)

questions.stem:
A human resources manager uses ChatGPT to help screen job applications. She notices that when she provides candidate names, the model's assessments seem to vary based on names that suggest particular ethnicities.

What does this MOST likely indicate and what should she do?

options.text: The model is correctly identifying cultural fit based on names; continue using it
The model may be reflecting societal biases present in its training data; remove names before submitting application content and monitor outputs for disparate treatment
This is a known bug; report it to the IT department
Use a different prompt — asking about "qualifications" instead of "candidates"

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because AI models can exhibit bias when demographic signals (names, locations, schools) are present in the input. Anonymizing inputs by removing names and other identifying information is the most direct mitigation, and ongoing monitoring is needed to catch disparate impact.

**A** is incorrect because varying assessments based on implied ethnicity constitutes discriminatory bias, not legitimate cultural fit evaluation.

**C** is incorrect because AI bias is a systemic property of the model's training, not a bug that an IT team can patch in an individual instance.

**D** is incorrect because reframing the question does not remove the names that trigger the biased response; removing the names does.

[OpenAI Usage Policies](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/safety-best-practices)

questions.stem:
A marketing copywriter publishes a social media campaign using content drafted by ChatGPT without any disclosure that AI was used.

Which responsible AI principle is MOST directly at risk in this scenario?

options.text: Transparency about the use of AI-generated content in public communications
Data minimization
Model version disclosure
Output length transparency

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because transparency — disclosing when content has been created or substantially assisted by AI — is a core responsible AI principle and is increasingly required by platform terms of service, regulatory guidance, and professional ethics standards.

**A** is incorrect because output length transparency is not a recognized responsible AI principle in this context.

**C** is incorrect because data minimization relates to personal data collection, not AI content disclosure.

**D** is incorrect because disclosing the specific model version used is not typically required; disclosing AI involvement is what matters.

[OpenAI Usage Policies](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/safety-best-practices)

questions.stem:
A customer service team lead asks ChatGPT to generate responses to customer complaints about billing disputes, and plans to send these responses directly to customers without any human review.

What is the MOST significant responsible AI concern with this workflow?

options.text: Customers may prefer human responses, making AI-drafted responses less effective
The model may use informal language in the responses
ChatGPT cannot write customer service emails
Removing human review from customer-facing communications about financial disputes risks sending inaccurate, inappropriate, or legally problematic responses

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because billing disputes carry financial and legal risk. An AI response that contains an incorrect refund amount, implies a legal commitment, or misreads the situation could escalate the dispute, create liability, or damage the customer relationship. Human review is a mandatory checkpoint in high-stakes communications.

**A** is incorrect because customer service email drafting is one of ChatGPT's most common use cases.

**C** is incorrect because customer preference is a secondary concern; accuracy and legal correctness are the primary issues.

**D** is incorrect because tone is a quality issue addressed through prompt design; it is not the primary responsible AI concern here.

[OpenAI Usage Policies](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/safety-best-practices)

questions.stem:
An IT security analyst wants to use ChatGPT to generate sample phishing email templates for use in an authorized internal security awareness training simulation.

What is the MOST appropriate approach?

options.text: Ask the model to generate real credential-harvesting links to make the simulation realistic
Submit the request directly with no additional context, as the intent is benign
Provide clear context in the prompt about the authorized training purpose, and review the output to ensure it is appropriate only for internal simulation and not repurposable for actual attacks
Avoid using AI for this use case entirely because phishing content is never permissible

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because authorized security awareness training is a legitimate use case. Providing explicit context about the purpose helps the model calibrate appropriately, and reviewing output before use ensures the content is suitable for training simulation rather than actual attacks.

**A** is incorrect because the model cannot verify intent, and contextless requests for phishing templates may be declined or produce less useful results.

**C** is incorrect because security awareness training with simulated phishing is a widely accepted practice; the key is authorized, controlled use.

**D** is incorrect because generating functional attack infrastructure (credential-harvesting links) would violate acceptable use policies even in a training context; realistic-looking but inert content is the appropriate approach.

[OpenAI Usage Policies](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/safety-best-practices)

questions.stem:
A junior analyst is impressed by ChatGPT's detailed explanation of a regulatory change and begins drafting a compliance report based entirely on the AI's interpretation without consulting the actual regulation text.

What responsible AI principle is being violated?

options.text: Data minimization
Appropriate reliance — using AI as a starting point that requires expert verification rather than as a definitive authority on regulatory interpretation
Response length guidelines
Model transparency

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because over-reliance on AI for regulatory interpretation is a core responsible AI risk. AI explanations of regulations can be subtly wrong, outdated, or jurisdiction-specific in ways that produce non-compliant reports. The regulation text and qualified legal or compliance professionals are the authoritative sources.

**A** is incorrect because data minimization concerns how much personal data is collected, not how much an AI output is trusted.

**C** is incorrect because there is no response length guideline being violated here.

**D** is incorrect because model transparency refers to disclosing AI use, not the degree of reliance on its output.

[OpenAI Usage Policies](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/safety-best-practices)

questions.stem:
A sales team member wants to paste a prospect's full LinkedIn profile — including personal details about education, prior companies, and location — into ChatGPT to generate a tailored pitch.

What privacy consideration should she address first?

options.text: Whether sharing personally identifiable information about an individual with a third-party AI service is consistent with applicable privacy laws and company data policies
Whether the AI model has been trained on LinkedIn data
Whether the prospect prefers email over LinkedIn outreach
Whether the prospect's LinkedIn profile uses a professional photo

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because submitting another individual's personal information to a third-party AI service raises privacy considerations under regulations such as GDPR and CCPA, and may violate company data handling policies. The professional should verify compliance before proceeding.

**A** is incorrect because the prospect's photo is irrelevant to the privacy question.

**C** is incorrect because whether the model was trained on LinkedIn data does not determine whether submitting the profile is permissible.

**D** is incorrect because outreach channel preference is a sales tactic consideration, not a privacy compliance issue.

[OpenAI Usage Policies](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/safety-best-practices)

questions.stem: An AI ethics committee is reviewing acceptable use standards for the company's ChatGPT deployment. Which TWO types of content requests should ALWAYS be declined based on OpenAI usage policies? (Select TWO)

options.text: Drafting fictional short stories with conflict and tension
Generating detailed instructions for creating biological weapons
Generating written summaries of internal meeting notes
Creating content that sexualizes minors

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because generating any sexual content involving minors (CSAM) is absolutely prohibited under OpenAI usage policies and applicable law worldwide.

**D** is correct because generating instructions for creating weapons capable of mass casualties — including biological, chemical, nuclear, and radiological weapons — is explicitly prohibited in OpenAI's usage policies regardless of stated purpose.

**A** is incorrect because summarizing meeting notes is a standard, permitted productivity use case.

**C** is incorrect because fiction with conflict and tension is permitted; only content that violates specific policy categories (explicit sexual content involving minors, CSAM, weapons of mass destruction instructions) is prohibited.

[OpenAI Usage Policies](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/safety-best-practices)

questions.stem:
A project coordinator needs to summarize a 45-minute recorded meeting into a one-page document with key decisions, action items, and owners.

Which prompting approach will MOST reliably produce this structured output?

options.text: Ask the model to bullet the entire transcript
Paste the transcript and ask "What happened in this meeting?"
Paste the transcript and ask for a structured summary with three explicit sections: Key Decisions, Action Items (with owner and due date), and Open Questions
Ask the model to write a summary without providing the transcript

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because specifying the exact sections (Key Decisions, Action Items with owner/due date, Open Questions) gives the model a precise output template that matches the coordinator's needs. This eliminates ambiguity about what "a one-page document" means.

**A** is incorrect because an open-ended question will produce a narrative summary that may not capture the structured data the coordinator needs.

**C** is incorrect because the model cannot know what was said without the transcript; it would generate a generic meeting summary template.

**D** is incorrect because converting the entire transcript to bullets produces a long, unfiltered document rather than a condensed summary.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
An executive assistant needs to prepare briefing notes for her CEO's meeting with a new partner company. She has a 20-page partner profile, three news articles about the company, and internal notes from a prior introduction call.

How should she structure her AI-assisted research process?

options.text: Submit all documents simultaneously and ask for a briefing
Summarize each source separately with the model, then ask it to synthesize the summaries into a briefing focused on the CEO's priorities and known discussion topics
Ask the model to find additional information about the partner company online
Skip the partner profile and only use the news articles since they are more current

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a layered approach — summarize each source, then synthesize with a specific lens (CEO priorities, discussion topics) — produces a focused briefing rather than a data dump. It also helps manage context window limits when dealing with multiple long documents.

**A** is incorrect because submitting all documents at once without a synthesis framework often results in a long, unfocused output that the assistant still needs to organize.

**C** is incorrect because standard ChatGPT does not browse the internet; and even when browsing is enabled, relying on AI to find unknown sources is less reliable than using provided materials.

**D** is incorrect because the partner profile contains structured, authoritative information that news articles cannot replace; recency does not substitute for depth.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A product team needs to write a pros and cons analysis of three cloud migration strategies to inform a board decision. A team member drafts an initial list with ChatGPT, but the board chair notes that the analysis seems to favor Strategy B without clear reasoning.

What should the team member do?

options.text: Ask the model to separate factual criteria from assumptions, score each strategy against explicitly defined criteria, and flag where data is incomplete
Switch to Strategy A to remove the appearance of bias
Remove the analysis from the board deck and present verbally instead
Ask the model to confirm that Strategy B is better

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the board chair's concern is about unexplained bias. Adding explicit, weighted criteria and asking the model to distinguish facts from assumptions creates transparency in the evaluation framework and allows the board to judge the analysis independently.

**A** is incorrect because asking the model to confirm a preference compounds the problem; it will generate justification for whatever position it has already taken.

**C** is incorrect because changing the recommendation based on appearance rather than evidence is the opposite of rigorous analysis.

**D** is incorrect because avoiding documentation reduces accountability; a well-structured written analysis is preferable to a verbal presentation for board-level decisions.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A team lead needs to send a follow-up email after a difficult conversation with a direct report about missed performance targets. She wants the email to be empathetic but clear about expectations.

What should she provide in the prompt for the BEST result?

options.text: Ask the model to write a "tough love" email without additional context
Copy and paste the employee's HR file into the prompt
The employee's performance data only
A brief summary of the conversation, the key message she wants the email to convey, the desired tone (empathetic but direct), and any agreed actions or follow-up steps

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because an effective email draft requires knowing the conversation context, the key message, the tone balance (empathy plus clarity), and the concrete outcomes. These inputs allow the model to calibrate language appropriately.

**A** is incorrect because data without conversational context produces a numbers-focused email rather than one that addresses the human dimensions of the situation.

**C** is incorrect because "tough love" is ambiguous; the model may produce an overly harsh email that damages the relationship rather than motivating improvement.

**D** is incorrect because an HR file contains highly sensitive personal information that should not be shared with external AI services.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A strategy consultant is using ChatGPT to synthesize insights from five 30-page market research reports into a single 3-page executive brief. After processing, she notices that the brief draws heavily from Report 3 and barely mentions findings from Reports 1 and 5.

What is the MOST likely cause and fix?

options.text: Reduce the target brief length to 1 page
Reports 1 and 5 were not attached correctly; resubmit them

When multiple long documents are in context, the model may weight content from the middle of the context window less heavily; process each report separately and ask the model to synthesize summaries with explicit coverage weighting
Report 3 contains more accurate data than the others; this is expected behavior

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because research shows large language models can underrepresent content from the middle of very long contexts. Processing each report separately into a summary, then synthesizing with explicit instructions to cover all five reports, ensures balanced coverage regardless of document position.

**A** is incorrect because the issue is not about documents missing from the submission; it is about how the model attends to content within a very long context.

**C** is incorrect because the model has no way to evaluate report accuracy independently; it does not preferentially cite more accurate sources.

**D** is incorrect because reducing the target length makes the coverage imbalance worse by providing less space for underrepresented reports.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A program manager needs to create a detailed project plan for a software migration. She asks ChatGPT to generate a project plan and receives a generic 5-phase waterfall plan with vague milestones.

Which additional inputs would MOST improve the output?

options.text: The company's org chart
The specific systems being migrated, the team size and roles, the target go-live date, known constraints, and any regulatory requirements
A description of what a project plan looks like
Ask the model to use an Agile methodology instead of waterfall

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because project plan quality is directly proportional to the specificity of the inputs. The systems involved, team composition, deadline, constraints, and regulatory environment are the variables that transform a generic template into an actionable plan.

**A** is incorrect because an org chart alone does not contain the project-specific information needed for meaningful milestones.

**C** is incorrect because the model knows what a project plan looks like; it needs project-specific information, not a format description.

**D** is incorrect because changing methodology without providing specific context still yields a generic Agile plan; the issue is input specificity, not methodology.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A sales operations analyst receives a dataset summary showing Q3 revenue by region and product line. She wants to explain the results to a non-technical sales team in plain language.

What should she ask ChatGPT to do?

options.text: Translate the key findings into plain-language narrative highlights, identifying the top performers and most notable changes, suitable for a sales team audience
Generate a new dataset based on the summary
Create a statistical regression model from the summary
Reproduce the data table in a different color scheme

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the task is audience translation — converting structured analytical data into plain narrative for a non-technical audience. Specifying the audience (sales team) and the desired output (highlights, top performers, notable changes) produces a useful briefing rather than raw analysis.

**A** is incorrect because color schemes are a visualization concern, not a communication task suited to a text AI.

**C** is incorrect because fabricating new data from a summary would produce inaccurate outputs for a factual analysis.

**D** is incorrect because statistical regression requires raw data, not a summary, and exceeds what is needed for plain-language communication.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem:
A policy writer needs to draft a company AI acceptable use policy. She asks ChatGPT to write the policy from scratch with no additional inputs.

What is the MOST significant limitation of this approach?

options.text: The policy will be too long for practical use
The model will generate the policy in legal language that employees cannot understand
ChatGPT cannot write policy documents

The model will generate a generic policy template that does not reflect the company's specific AI tools, risk profile, industry regulations, or cultural values — significant customization will be required

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a policy generated without company-specific context — which AI tools are deployed, what data is handled, the industry's regulatory environment, and the organization's risk tolerance — will be a generic template requiring substantial rework before adoption.

**A** is incorrect because policy drafting is a common and productive use case for ChatGPT.

**C** is incorrect because length is a stylistic issue that can be addressed with a prompt instruction; it is not the primary limitation.

**D** is incorrect because the model can produce plain-language policies; the issue is content specificity, not language register.

[OpenAI Text Generation Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation)

questions.stem: A business analyst wants to automatically export weekly sales data from a CRM system into a ChatGPT workflow for analysis. Which feature would enable ChatGPT to retrieve live data from the CRM without manual copy-pasting?

options.text: Using a custom GPT with no external connections
Increasing the ChatGPT context window
Configuring a ChatGPT Action that connects to the CRM's API
Uploading a PDF version of the CRM data each week

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because ChatGPT Actions allow a custom GPT to call external APIs — including CRM APIs — to retrieve live data without manual export. The Action is configured with an OpenAPI schema that describes the CRM endpoints.

**A** is incorrect because context window size determines how much text can be processed in a single request; it does not create an API connection to external systems.

**C** is incorrect because uploading a PDF each week is the manual copy-paste approach the analyst is trying to avoid.

**D** is incorrect because a custom GPT without external connections cannot retrieve live CRM data.

[OpenAI Agents Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agents)

questions.stem:
A customer experience team has deployed a custom GPT for handling tier-1 support inquiries. The team wants to ensure that the GPT never discusses competitor products.

Where should this restriction be configured?

options.text: In a follow-up message after each customer inquiry
In the system prompt or custom GPT instructions defined by the operator
In the customer's user profile settings
As a post-processing filter applied to responses before delivery

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the operator — the team deploying the custom GPT — controls the system prompt and custom instructions. This is the appropriate layer for persistent behavioral constraints like topic restrictions that apply to all users of the GPT.

**A** is incorrect because a restriction delivered after each inquiry means the GPT could discuss competitors before the restriction is applied; it also scales poorly.

**C** is incorrect because user profile settings do not typically include GPT behavioral restrictions; and users should not be responsible for enforcing operator-defined boundaries.

**D** is incorrect because post-processing filters can be effective but are a second-line defense; the system prompt is the appropriate primary control point.

[OpenAI Agents Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agents)

questions.stem:
A development team is evaluating whether to use an AI agent to automatically update customer account records in a database or to use ChatGPT directly to suggest updates for a human to review and apply.

Which scenario is BEST suited for the autonomous agent approach?

options.text: Closing customer accounts based on payment delinquency decisions
Updating customer credit scores based on AI risk assessment
Any database update, since AI agents are faster than human review
High-volume, low-risk updates such as automatically correcting address formatting errors when source data confidence is high

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because high-volume, low-risk, high-confidence operations — like formatting corrections — are ideal for automation. The cost of an error is low, the correction is verifiable, and automation provides clear efficiency gains.

**B** is incorrect because account closure decisions carry significant customer impact, regulatory obligations, and dispute risk; these require human review and appropriate process controls.

**C** is incorrect because credit score updates have direct financial impact on customers and may require regulated decision-making processes including human oversight.

**D** is incorrect because risk level, reversibility, and regulatory requirements determine when automation is appropriate — not simply speed.

[OpenAI Agents Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agents)

questions.stem:
A technology team has built an AI agent that drafts and sends customer emails. During testing, they discover that occasionally the agent sends emails with incorrect product pricing. They do not want to add a human-in-the-loop step because it slows the workflow.

What is the MOST appropriate technical mitigation?

options.text: Rewrite the agent's prompt to instruct it not to make pricing mistakes
Send the emails only on weekdays when the team can monitor more easily
Accept the pricing errors as an acceptable rate of failure for agent workflows
Add an automated validation step that checks prices in the draft against a live pricing database before sending, and route flagged emails to a human queue

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because an automated validation step — programmatically checking prices against a live source before sending — adds a quality gate without the latency of manual review. Routing only flagged emails to humans provides targeted oversight where errors are detected, maintaining throughput for correct emails.

**A** is incorrect because pricing errors in customer communications create customer trust issues, potential contractual obligations, and financial risk; they cannot be accepted as normal failure.

**C** is incorrect because instructing the model not to make mistakes does not prevent hallucinated prices from entering the draft; structural validation is needed.

**D** is incorrect because scheduling does not prevent errors and does not reduce the number of incorrect emails sent.

[OpenAI Agents Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agents)

questions.stem:
A business process manager wants to chain multiple AI tasks: extract action items from meeting notes, assign each to a team member, and draft a Slack message notifying each person of their task.

What is the MOST efficient architecture for this workflow?

options.text: Use ChatGPT only for the drafting step and do extraction and assignment manually
Run a single ChatGPT prompt that does all three tasks simultaneously

Create a sequential pipeline where the output of each step (extracted items, assigned items, drafted messages) is the input to the next, with a validation checkpoint between assignment and notification
Have team members manually review meeting notes and assign their own tasks

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because sequential pipelines with intermediate validation catch errors before they propagate. A validation step between assignment and notification is particularly important — if assignments are wrong, sending incorrect Slack notifications to team members would create confusion that is difficult to retract.

**A** is incorrect because a single prompt for three interdependent tasks typically produces less accurate results than specialized prompts for each step, and offers no checkpoint for catching errors mid-flow.

**C** is incorrect because manual processing defeats the automation benefit the manager is seeking.

**D** is incorrect because partial automation loses efficiency gains; the entire workflow benefits from AI assistance with appropriate checkpoints.

[OpenAI Agents Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agents)

questions.stem:
A development team is using the OpenAI Responses API to build a coding assistant that maintains conversation history for a user session. As sessions grow long, the team notices increasing latency and cost.

What is the MOST effective strategy for managing this?

options.text: Limit sessions to 10 messages and force users to start over
Implement a context management strategy that summarizes earlier turns and retains only the most recent exchanges and relevant code snippets in the active context
Switch to a model with a smaller context window to reduce processing time
Send only the most recent user message without any conversation history

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because summarizing earlier turns compresses historical context while retaining semantic continuity, and retaining recent exchanges plus relevant code preserves the specific technical context the assistant needs. This reduces token count — and therefore cost and latency — without degrading the user experience.

**A** is incorrect because forcing session restarts loses conversational context that is valuable for a coding assistant; users would need to re-explain their code context.

**C** is incorrect because a smaller context window constrains the amount of information that can be processed and would cause the application to break for long sessions, not improve performance.

**D** is incorrect because sending only the most recent message loses all conversational context, making the assistant unable to understand references to previous steps or code defined earlier.

[OpenAI Responses API](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/tools)

questions.stem:
A product manager is deploying a custom GPT for use by her company's employees. She wants employees to be able to ask follow-up questions within a session but does not want them to override the GPT's core instructions.

What correctly describes the relationship between operator and user controls in this scenario?

options.text: The operator sets system-level instructions that take precedence over user requests; users can adjust behavior within the limits the operator permits
The product manager must approve each employee conversation in real time
Operators and users have equal authority over GPT behavior
Users can override operator system prompt instructions if they ask politely

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the operator-user hierarchy in ChatGPT deployments grants operators (like the product manager) control over system-level instructions that govern the GPT's scope, persona, and restrictions. Users can interact flexibly within that defined scope but cannot override core operator constraints.

**A** is incorrect because user messages that attempt to override operator instructions should be resisted by the GPT; politeness does not constitute an authorization mechanism.

**C** is incorrect because operator control is implemented through system prompts and configuration, not through real-time conversation approval.

**D** is incorrect because operators and users explicitly have different authority levels; operator configuration takes precedence.

[OpenAI Agents Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agents)

questions.stem: A team is designing quality control checkpoints for an AI agent that autonomously processes and responds to customer refund requests. Which TWO checkpoints would MOST effectively reduce risk in this pipeline? (Select TWO)

options.text: Trusting the agent entirely since AI approval accuracy exceeds human accuracy in high-volume environments
Automatically escalating refund requests that trigger edge-case flags (e.g., unusually high amounts, repeated requests from the same account) to human review
An automated rule check that verifies the refund amount is within policy limits before the agent triggers a transaction
A random daily log review by a team member to catch systematic errors the automated checks miss

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a programmatic policy check before any financial transaction executes is a structural safeguard that catches out-of-policy refunds before they are issued — a critical checkpoint in any financial workflow.

**D** is correct because edge cases (high amounts, repeated requests, anomalous patterns) are where agent errors are most likely and most costly. Escalating these to humans provides targeted oversight without slowing the majority of straightforward cases.

**B** is partially useful but is incorrect as a primary checkpoint because random sampling catches only a fraction of errors and operates after the fact — errors have already occurred before the log review happens.

**C** is incorrect because automation accuracy claims do not eliminate the need for oversight, particularly in financial workflows where individual errors can be significant and systematic biases can cause widespread harm.

[OpenAI Agents Guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agents)
